sentences.text: Yes.
That day in the morning, they told us [BACKROUND NOISE] that we were not going to work this morning, and then we saw a lot of wagons arrive-- horse-driven wagons.
The farmers were required to bring the-- the wagons over.
Sorry about that again.
And then we were put on the wagons, and mothers and daughters together.
But my younger brother was still-- oh, there's something in there I forgot.
Something before that.
What still happened.
Can I go back to Staw?
Please go back.
No problem.
Because there once-- there were still a few children in Staw with the families.
And one day, they cleared out the camp because they didn't go with us to work.
And they took all the children away.
While we were working, we heard a lot of shooting.
And then we found out from the farmers-- they were telling us while we were working.
Even one day a farmer brought us some bread and other food because the policeman from Siedliszcze recognized the bus.
And he sent for us some food, but he couldn't bring the food himself.
And then that same farmer told that there was a lot of getting the people out from the forest and from the camp.
And they were executed.
And amongst them, there was a little boy, about seven years old.
And he was from western Poland, from Poznan.
And the father got killed the beginning of the war.
He was in the Polish military.
And that little boy could speak very well English.
And when the-- the ditches were-- were dug, the boy jumped in first-- that was what we were told-- and he said to them in German, if you want to shoot me, I want to be first.
And in that-- amongst those people, there were the grandmother and grandfather of my parent-- my father.
That day they were somewhere, I don't know where they were taken.
But when we were still in-- in-- but that was after.
That was in--
They were gone.
All the children were gone.
But my youngest brother managed to be outside the mill somewhere.
He was trying to-- to try-- not to hide, but to find some wood.
He wanted to boil a kettle of water so when we get home from work, we'll have some hot water.
And in that time, he-- they came and took all the-- the-- the sick people, and the people who couldn't work anymore, and the children.
They were all taken away.
And--
All of us were still together.
No, no, no, no, no.
No.
Later on I found out what happened to them, when we were-- when I escaped and I got back home.
No, but in-- in Staw, it was the parents of your father, your grandparents.
No, I don't know where they were.
They were maybe hiding in the forest.
I don't know where they were.
They were not.
No, no, no, no.
They were not with us, no.
Yes, they were taken.
All the old people were taken out after they were-- not out, but taken away to the cemetery-- the Jewish cemetery and they were executed.
Afterwards, yes.
After this camp.
Oh, yes.
My mother was still with me, yes.
No, no.
They were not in-- in-- in the camp.
They were not-- they were still somewhere at home, hiding, because all the younger people were taking away.
When we left Siedliszcze for the camp, for that-- [BACKROUND NOISE] to Staw, the elder people were left there.
And then they were executed.
But we-- I didn't know.
We didn't know straight away what happened to them.
Only later I found out.
No, no.
In Siedliszcze, yes.
That morning, it was a very, very bleak morning in Staw, and we were-- it was-- the whole world was covered in snow.
But somehow the-- the sky looked a bit red.
We couldn't understand it.
I still can see the red sky in the middle of the winter.
And we were all on the wagons, like my mother, my sister, and the youngest brother.
And my other brother was with my father.
And we were driving along.
And then when we came to a crossroad, it was-- to the right was to Chelm and to the left to Siedliszcze or vice versa.
And when the wagons were turning to Vlodava-- not Siedliszcze, Wlodawa.
Sorry.
To Wlodawa.
My youngest brother, who was on the same wagon with me and my sister, he said-- we heard certain things on what was going on in Sobibor.
But that was-- we thought it was only rumors.
But I don't know from where he knew maybe more than we did.
He said to my mother, let's say goodbye to the night, because we won't see this-- the daylight.
My youngest.
He was only 11 then.
And when we come to Sobibor, it was about 8 o'clock at night.
And the whole camp was lit.
It was like daylight.
The-- music was playing.
And we were told to get off the-- the wagons.
The wagons drove away.
The men separate, so my youngest brother went to-- with my father, and I stayed with my sister and mother.
And then a big, tall German came along and stand on a-- on-- on-- like a platform.
And he said that, who can knit?
And my mother pushed me out.
She said, you a good knitter.
And I said, you know, Friedl could come, too.
She said no, I want to stay with-- with Mom.
And there were other girls come out. 12 of us was taken out from that transport to do the knitting.
That barrack where we were going to stay wasn't even finished yet.
So we were just huddled in all in a corner.
We heard a lot of noises going on, like-- like machinery working, or-- or-- or some electrical-- you know-- know-- electricity.
It was-- I don't know what the noise was.
And we waited till the morning, and then the German came along.
He told us to-- to go and get some-- like a slice of bread and a bit of coffee.
And then we helped him to finish off our barrack, where we were going to stay.
They put a little stove in, like a potbelly, because we couldn't knit-- knit with cold fingers.
And he took us to a-- a big shed where there were shoes.
And he told us, you know, if you want to choose shoes from that heap, we can.
And he brought over to me a pair of boots.
And that was my sister's boots.
So I told him they were too small for me, or too big.
I made an excuse because I had-- my boots were still all right.
It was winter.
We still had things to wear.
Then we knew what happened.
And we stayed.
I knitted there till-- till spring.
We had to undo a lot of cardigans or jumpers, very good wool we had to undo.
And then we had to make it out like new wool.
And then we had to knit a-- a knee sock for soldier a day.
Yes.
Yes.
In that barrack, yes.
And we were knitting.
Not all of us could knit.
Some of us had to prepare the wool.
There was a-- a lady-- oh, the moment-- Schildkraut was her name.
She was there with her son and a smaller daughter because her son was a very, very good knitter.
And-- and that was the only people beside us young ones, 17, 18 years old.
The-- only that woman was a mother with her son and a daughter.
Nobody else was-- only 12 of us all together.
Nobody was in charge.
They just told us what to do.
They gave us approximate-- how big a man's sock has to be and how long it has to be.
And then they brought in a lot of wool.
We had to undo-- unravel everything.
And one like the little girl, Irene, she helped with her mother.
She was a good knitter, too, and helped do up, you know, the skeins of wool so we could make the socks.
And when we couldn't finish it at daylight, we used to save that little-- that fuel they were giving us for the stove at night.
So when they switched the lights off, so we opened the little doors.
And by the flicker of the-- of the burning stove, we used to finish our socks.
Some of us were faster or slower.
So one used to help out.
Some of them were really very, very fast to knit the-- the foot, and some of them were very fast to knit the top of the sock.
So we used to-- to-- but we had to give up every day almost a-- a half dozen pairs.
We met-- we met the requirement.
We isn't-- did-- one day, you know, Mira, she said we have to knit something for-- for [?
Wagner. ?] Maybe he will be a little bit better.
Maybe he will give us a little bit more food or something.
But that day we didn't know how beastly that [?
Wagner ?] was.
And we managed in-- in between to knit a sleeveless pullover for him.
Yes, yes.
And he-- Mira went to-- to give him that.
And he told her-- even she could speak very well German.
She was from western Poland.
I think maybe near Krakow somewhere.
And he-- he said, you better run away before I-- I harm you.
He didn't even give a thank you or anything.
And later on, we saw him wearing that sleeveless jumper.
Yes.
But when the spring came, we had to wind up the-- the knitting place.
And we were taken-- I was taken to do the sorting.
We knew then what happened.
Not the Germans, but the people who were already there, working there.
They knew what was happening, and they told us.
Oh, yes.
Yes, when we were-- were for the roll call.
We were counted.
And when we were coming back from work and when we're given something to eat before we start work, 5 o'clock in the morning or 4 o'clock sometimes in the summer.
In the winter, a little bit later because the days were shorter.
We had to all stand the roll call and be counted.
And when it was the most coldest night, they taught us German songs because we had to sing going to work.
So we were singing Polish songs, and the others were singing maybe Dutch songs.
No, they all wanted us to sing German songs.
So in the coldest nights, we had to stand on the ice and snow.
Outside, yes.
And somebody there who knew those songs used to teach us enough they heard us sing, that it's all right.
So they let us go.
And then from then on, we had to sing the German songs.
You wore, yes.
We didn't get any camp clothes.
We had to wear our own clothes and look after-- after it so it wouldn't get torn.
Or-- or-- you know, but when we were sorting, sometimes, you know, we could manage to-- to have something for underwear and all that.
But otherwise, you know, we just had to make sure that ours stretch a long way.
Oh, yes.
There was three like bunks.
And we were divided.
But we even made it that way that we slept more in the middle and down-- down, the bottom one, because the roof was like-- like to a point, and I was going to sleep upstairs.
And when I turned my-- my head to the roof, the head was freezing.
When I turn my-- so the-- the girls got together, you know, and they-- they slept so close together that four of us fitted in the-- in the bottom part, the middle and the bottom part.
So--
No, no, no.
That was not-- not far.
That-- not far from the toilet there was a little fence, and so there we could see how they were taking the people from the trains to the gas chambers, naked.
Yes.
And not only that.
We knew the crematorium because 24 hours a day, it was burning, and smoke, and all that.
That was our air we were breathing.
Burning flesh.
No, the red in the sky was over there, still in Staw.
That has nothing to do with-- with Sobibor.
It was just the daylight and the sky was red.
And then the--
Knitting went to sorting.
But our little barrack was confiscated, and we slept with all the other girls and women in a big barrack.
Yes.
Because it was-- summer was coming.
They didn't need it anymore.
And then with sorting, and one day I picked up a bundle.
And there was a winter jacket, a brown checked jacket.
And I told the girls who were standing beside me, and we were all together, and I said, that is my mother's jacket.
And they said, oh, that was probably gone a long time ago.
And I said, no, I can prove it to you.
So I just managed to hid it somewhere so the Germans won't see what I was doing.
And I said, later on, when he won't be around, I'll undo a little pocket on top of the jacket and I'll prove that it is my mother's.
She sew her wedding ring into that pocket.
And it was there.
Yes.
Oh, no.
No, no.
I had to give it up, and the ring I buried in the-- in the sand.
I didn't give him the ring.
Yes.
So from then on, after the sorting, we were taken to the-- work in the forest.
We had to cut branches.

Men were cutting the branches off the trees, and we had to get the branches away because they were making-- not-- not roads, but ways in the forest so a horseman can ride through, so he wouldn't get caught from the branches.
And when that was done, we were taken to-- not-- what is it called?
Not army, but ammunition bunkers.
They had in the forest built bunkers where they had a lot of ammunition from the eastern front.
But only the roofs were sticking out.
Everything was in the ground.
We had to clean the ammunition.

Sometimes when there used to come some trains, we had to unload trains with calcium or-- I don't know what you actually call it in-- in English-- because they needed it for the-- what they couldn't cremate.
The-- the bodies had to be buried, so they had to put some disinfectant on top.
I know it in Polish.
It's called 'vapno.' When it is raw, you put water to it and it boils.
It burns.
Quick lime.
Or we used to whitewash with it, you know, when they didn't have people in-- in-- a lot of houses used to be whitewashed with that.
And then in the winter-- that was in the summer.
And we were not even allowed to put rags on our hands when we were unloading it.
And it burned our skin sometime to the bone.
And in the winter, I don't know what they needed for the iron baths.
We had to unload iron baths in the winter.
And you know how iron is frozen, the skin used to stick to it.
But that's how it used to be with them.
Used to take us away from anywhere we were working when a train arrived with something we had to do, to help out with unloading.
It wasn't enough the men who were doing it, so we all had to help.
And then from-- from--
Oh, yes, we were not allowed to be sick.
The sick people were taken to the third camp.
That's where the gas chambers, or they were executed before even that, shot.
One day when I was still sorting, a-- a boy-- call him a boy, he maybe was 17 or 18.
He hid a can of sardines.
And one of the Scharfuhrers, one of the Germans, saw it.
He took him out and shot him.
He said that's only for the German army.
We were not allowed.
Even when we used to find something, that-- like biscuits, or people were bringing food with them.
They were coming from all over.
And we had to so discreetly put things into our mouth, not to chew, because if they'd see that, that was the end.
We were not allowed to do that.
Oh, no, no.
I wouldn't-- I didn't want to hide it.
I didn't want-- I knew, you know, if they found it-- and-- And the-- the only-- the-- the-- transports who used to come from part of Europe.
They were most in cattle train.
But from France, the transport, they used to come in passenger trains.
And they were still nicely dressed up, and they even had suitcases.
And they even tried to give some money for the people who were working on there.
They were called the Bahnhofkommando.
They were helping the people to get off of the trains.
They-- they were not clobbered like from other parts of Europe, the people.
And they climbed down with the suitcases.
They were even trying to pay for the ones who helped them, give them some money.
And they were told to put the suitcase-- they were given numbers.
They were told put the suitcases on a place.
And they undressed, and they were going for-- for health reasons to-- to have a shower.
And that's it.
They never came back.
That was the shower.
Yes.
Oh, there was a lot, a lot of clothes, open suitcases.
And so the good clothes were separate.
They were straight away bundled up and they went to Germany.
And then all the jewelry, there was special places where that was put.
Oh, they were like-- like-- you know, all over the place.
And somebody didn't work fast enough or didn't-- I learned how to tie up parcels there and all that, because that was a special end of the big, big barrack where we used to put all the bundles there.
And the good ones were straight away differently-- new stuff was always, you know, for Germany.
Yes, yes.
No hospitals, no.
But the only one, we had a-- like a nurse.
But that was a man nurse, we used to call him, the sanitator.
He was a Czechoslovakian from Slovakia or Czechoslovakia-- I don't know.
His name is Kurt Thomas.
He lives in the United States.
And he used to help us bandage, I think, if you injured yourself or somebody cut sometimes doing work.
That was the only thing they were allowed to have.
You know, but not actually be sick.
But later on there was-- when-- after the sorting, I was taken to the German wash laundry.
All Jewish people.
Yes.
The men and the women that you met were all Jewish.
Yeah, all Jewish.
Yes.
And in the laundry there one day, I got up and I felt-- not sick, but it was very bad.
I had middle-ear infection.
But I couldn't tell anybody that I-- I was sick.
And we were waiting for the water to-- to heat up for the washing because everything had to be manual done, the washing, and boiled, and all that.
And I didn't see the other girls.
There were four girls in the laundry.
They didn't see the German coming in the laundry, and I wasn't fast enough.
I couldn't work even then because the water wasn't hot.
But my ear was aching so badly, so I was standing near that-- that where the copper was, where the water was heating.
And he took me out from that-- from the laundry to that [?
Wagner. ?] And [?
Wagner ?] was on the place where they had all the wood for the crematoriums.
And that was called the holzplatz.
And [?
Wagner ?] was there.
And he said to him, but in German, what shall we do with that little one?
He told me to bend down and to lift up even my skirt.
And he was such a tall fellow.
And there, what do you call, the-- the whips, they were steel inside with leather on top.
And the whip was so long, nearly-- you know, when he's starting to hit me.
If I would have been taller, maybe I would have got it more on my backside.
But being so short, I got it most in the waist.
So every time the whip came around to the front, later on I had blisters.
And I didn't know that-- then that something happened, you know, to my kidney.
And when I got back to work, the girls helped me to put cold compresses.
I was so sore.
I couldn't sit.
I couldn't lie.
But somehow I managed.
And then we had a dentist.
And then I was complaining about the ear.
And I thought to say that I have got a toothache.
And he pushed through my eardrum, and that I got the relief from the middle-ear infection.
And I wasn't-- I wasn't taken away.
Oh, it took a few days.
But we didn't have time to feel sorry for ourselves.
One have to go to work just the same.
But-- but-- in the laundry.
But the girls were so good.
We were teams.
We were all the same age.
And when they saw what's happening, one was always looking out the window to see if the German didn't come, so, you know, I wouldn't have to work so hard.
Because there are certain things we had to do in a day.
It wasn't just leave it for tomorrow.
And after that, a laundry was closed up because they took laundry-- the German laundry-- out to the German quarters.
And they got some Ukraine girls or something, they were working then.
But I was transferred to do laundry-- the Jewish laundry.
And that was so hard.
Oh, gosh.
I thought I wouldn't last long in that laundry.
All the time with-- with the water, and the wet, and the steam.
And-- and I don't know why we had to work there.
What-- what--
Boiling the shirts because they wanted us to-- to keep clean.
We had sheets over our straw in the-- in the-- where we were sleeping.
That has to be washed and all that.
Pardon me.
Yes.
Frenzel was a Sturmbannfuhrer who was in charge when [?
Wagner ?] was on holidays.
And he came in and asked.
You know.
And I said, I can't make a whole shirt, but during the war I learned somewhere to make collars and cuffs.
Excuse me.
[DRINKS] And he said, that's what he needs, for collars and cuffs.
And my dear, that was from hell to heaven.
I would not have last long in the laundry.
But going to a sewing room, the German side of the camp, was nice and-- and clean.
And doing sewing was a lot easier then.
And from time to time, a German, when he was going on leave, he used to come in and ask could we make him, like for, dress up a doll or something for his wife, to take home, and all that.
That was done beside what we had to do.
No, no, no.
I was the--
No.
I knew the other girls who  were in the sewing room, but we didn't work together before.
And they are-- you know, the Germans sometimes, when he had a little bit of heart on the right side, used to bring in maybe a piece of bread.
One once brought in a piece of cake and a few cubes of sugar.
And we didn't know.
The cake, we looked at it, but didn't know to eat it or look at it and something like that.
But the sugar, I-- I put away.
I didn't eat the sugar.
But later when we escaped, I had the sugar with me.
It was one that was the [INAUDIBLE] lady.
I just have to-- to think about it.
I think four or five.
But that lady who was the [INAUDIBLE] lady, she was a very able person.
We had to embroider monograms for hankies, and monograms on under-- other things.
It wasn't all the time at the machine sewing.
But it was really, really different.
We were not rushed there.
We were told what we had to do, and it had to be done proper.
And it was different.
And then-- I-- I got on my feet there again.
Oh, yes.
Oh, yes.
We were escorted back-- food-- to have our food, and we were, again, roll-called, counted, and all that.
But I was-- I worked there till the escape.
No.
No, but one, I saw it in the film.
I didn't know about it.
But that could've been before us, because the camp was there since May '42.
And we were brought over there-- I always say 'we' because we were coming, not-- not just one person, me-- in-- in December.
So the few months before, what happened there, I don't know.
But one day-- I can't remember what month it was-- there was-- they called out all the Dutch workers.
It was about 70 or maybe more.
And they were taken away to the gas chambers.
They actually called them out--
Called them out.
Called them out.
They knew who were Dutch, and they called them out.
There was a mother and a son from the Dutch people, and he was taken away, too.
All the men, not the Dutch women.
And she begged him to-- to-- to leave her son.
And oh, that was so tragic there.
But there was a young man in the bakery.
But he was a Polish boy, Polish Jew.
And he stepped out from the line, and he said-- will it be-- I don't know how he said it-- he wanted to go in place of that Dutch-- Dutch boy.
And he-- they took him, and they left the Dutch boy with the mother.
Oh, later on, I don't know, when we escaped.
But that young man gave his life for that-- he could have survived because he was Polish.
He could speak.

Because all the Polish Jews was-- had a better chance of survival than-- than-- And then one day, they brought over a machine to dig-- to dig trenches because they didn't have enough room for the crematorium.
And we had to-- to take the soil away from the digging machine on a heap with all the [INAUDIBLE] we could dig up.
And there was a Dutch boy.
He was-- we used to call him Caruso.
A beautiful voice.
And that Frenzel didn't like him.
Why?
Because they told us, you know, the musicians had sometimes to play, and who can sing had to sing.
We had to-- you know, they used to come and listen or just amuse themselves.
And he didn't like the Dutch boy, that Caruso.
So each time that boy used to come-- he was maybe about 17.
He used to come around with that [INAUDIBLE] of soil.
He hit him, and he was beaten that hard that he didn't survive the night.
In the morning, he was-- he was gone.
It was so sad, so cruel, because everybody loved him.
He was such good looking, and he still wasn't so thin like others because he wasn't there very long.
And to a lot of us, you know, happened, if he didn't like somebody.
When [?
Wagner ?] had night duties, he needed every morning when he used to come after the-- it was finished-- 5 o'clock or 6 o'clock in the morning-- with his big Shepherd dog.
He used to pull out for no reason somebody from the sleeping quarters and execute.
Not-- not-- not always in front.
Just behind the big gates, or take him away.
The slightest thing that he didn't like that person did, he wasn't living anymore.
Oh, that [?
Wagner. ?] We haven't got any words how-- how he-- how he was.
And then the 23rd of September, '42, a transport of ration POWs were brought in.
They were still in there from [?
Kharkov, ?] maybe another city in Russia.
They were still in their military uniforms.
[BACKROUND NOISE] And amongst them was a lieutenant with the name of Alexander Pechersky.
I think my-- no.
I could maybe write it down for you.
But it's all in the books.
He was so known.
We-- we used to call him Sasha.
And he was stationed there, you know, with all the other workers.
They were working a lot of other places.
And he was-- he heard what was going on in that camp.
And one day he heard a little girl screaming.
And he had at home in Russia a three-years-old daughter.
And the people-- the boys in the camp before that transport arrived, we were already planning an escape because a lot of them wanted.
Single escapes were never good because they used to caught them.
And besides that, they always used to have-- 10 or 20 more used to be executed.
But before that-- I just-- you know, before that-- that was in-- in the summer, June or July.
There was a [INAUDIBLE] kommando.
They were working outside in the forest.
And they were going to fetch water, and they didn't come back-- two of them.
They were caught, and the whole [INAUDIBLE] kommando was brought to-- they used to call it sportplatz.
And we all have to stand in a half-circle to witness that the whole group, 12 or 14, were executed.
And--
Yes.
They were even watching us, when we had our heads down.
It wasn't good.
We had to watch what happened.
That's it, if--
